title: Painting woodwork to a gloss finish
subtitle: Preparation is the finish
excerpt: How to achieve a smooth gloss finish on interior woodwork: rubbing back the existing surface, applying an undercoat, sanding between coats, and applying a gloss or satin topcoat without brush marks or sags.
sourceNotes: Dulux and Ronseal technical data sheets for water-based and oil-based gloss. Collins Complete DIY Manual woodwork painting chapter. City & Guilds Painting and Decorating Level 2 syllabus.

recipeTools.notes: For removing any loose or flaking paint before sanding.

aliases: gloss finish on doors and trim

content.text: Gloss paint reveals every imperfection because its sheen reflects light at a low angle across the surface. The quality of the finish depends almost entirely on the preparation: a surface that is smooth, clean, and primed will take a brush-mark-free topcoat. A rough or greasy surface will not produce a good result regardless of paint quality.
Preparation
Step 1, clean the surface.
Wash the woodwork with sugar soap solution to remove grease and grime. Rinse with clean water and allow to dry fully. Grease prevents paint bonding and causes fisheye defects in the dried film.
Step 2, rub down the existing paint.

Sand the surface with 120-grit on the sanding block, working along the grain. The aim is to abrade the gloss surface to give the new coat a key, and to level any nibs or brush marks from the last coat. Wipe the dust off with a tack cloth or barely damp cloth.
Step 3, repair and prime bare areas.

Where the old paint has been sanded through to bare wood, fill any holes with wood filler and allow to cure. Prime bare wood with the appropriate primer for the topcoat. Let it dry, sand lightly with 180-grit, and wipe clean.
Undercoat
Step 4, apply an undercoat.

For any surface going from a dark colour to white, or from bare wood, an undercoat provides opacity and improves adhesion. Brush the undercoat on in long strokes along the length of the wood, then lay off the final strokes with a nearly dry brush from end to end without lifting the brush. Allow to dry.
Step 5, fine-sand the undercoat.

Sand the dried undercoat with 180-grit to remove any dust nibs. The surface should feel perfectly smooth to the touch. Wipe clean. This sanding step is the most important for the quality of the topcoat.
Topcoat
Step 6, apply the first topcoat.

Do not stir gloss paint: stirring introduces bubbles that appear as fisheyes in the dried film. Turn the tin upside down for a few minutes to remix, then open and use without stirring. Load the brush to about half the bristle length. Apply in long strokes with the grain, then cross strokes to even the coverage, then finish with light strokes along the grain from end to end. Do not go back over paint that has begun to set.
Step 7, apply the second topcoat.

Allow the first topcoat to dry for the time stated on the tin, then sand lightly with 180-grit to remove nibs, wipe clean, and apply the second coat in the same way. Two coats of topcoat over an undercoat is the standard for a durable high-gloss finish.
Common mistakes
Brush marks showing in the dried gloss.

The lay-off strokes were too heavy, or the paint was too thick. Water-based gloss can be thinned slightly with a teaspoon of water per 100 ml; this extends the flow-out time. For oil-based gloss, thin with white spirit very sparingly.
Runs and sags on vertical surfaces.
Too much paint applied in one coat, or the laying-off strokes were omitted. Sand back runs with 120-grit once fully dry, re-prime if needed, and re-apply a thinner coat.
Fisheye spots in the dried film.
Caused by grease, silicone contamination, or bubbles from stirring the tin. Sand back the affected area, degrease with white spirit, and re-coat. Do not stir gloss.
